title: Erdogan Leverages NATO Summit to Push Defense Ambitions, F-35 Comeback; Imamoglu Ejected From Court
subhead: Turkey's president used the NATO Ankara Summit to announce a $24 billion 'Steel Dome' air defense project and press for the lifting of allied defense-industry restrictions — including CAATSA sanctions — while Trump signaled he would remove sanctions and consider F-35 sales. Meanwhile, Istanbul Mayor Imamoglu was ejected from his corruption trial on the same day world leaders gathered in the capital.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: Erdogan's Stage
sections.body:
The 36th NATO Heads of State and Government Summit convened in Ankara on July 7-8, the first time Turkey has hosted a full NATO summit in years. President Recep Tayyip Erdogan used the platform to make two significant announcements: a $24 billion allocation for a 'Steel Dome' air and missile defense project, and a call for NATO allies to lift all defense-industry restrictions among members — a clear reference to the CAATSA sanctions the US imposed on Turkey in 2020 for purchasing Russian S-400 systems.

Erdogan stated that Turkey had 'already reached a budget share of 1.5%' in security-related expenditures and set a goal of reaching 3.5% of GDP in defense spending by 2030, five years ahead of the alliance's 2035 target. He also announced plans to establish a NATO-accredited Center of Excellence for Countering Unmanned Systems, citing Turkey's real-battlefield experience with UAVs and UCAVs. According to the Ilkha news agency, Erdogan said Turkey would fulfill nearly all 361 capability targets assigned by NATO within three years, ahead of schedule.

Defense Minister Yasar Guler, speaking at a side event organized by SETA and the Munich Security Conference, was more pointed: 'Excluding Turkey will not make Europe safer.' He argued that European defense initiatives must be open to all NATO members regardless of EU membership and that NATO-EU cooperation should be 'inclusive, integrative, and mutually reinforcing.'
sections.whyItMatters: Erdogan turned the host role into a platform to legitimize Turkey's demand for defense-industry parity — specifically, the removal of sanctions that block F-35 access. The Steel Dome announcement signals Turkey intends to build its own layered air defense rather than remain dependent on allies, while the 3.5% GDP spending target positions Turkey as meeting and exceeding NATO's new spending bar.

sections.heading: Trump Signals F-35 Sale to Turkey, Pledges to Lift CAATSA Sanctions
sections.body:
The biggest bilateral development of the summit was President Donald Trump's explicit statement that the US would lift CAATSA sanctions on Turkey and consider allowing the sale of F-35 fighter jets. Speaking alongside Erdogan at the presidential compound on July 7, Trump said: 'We're going to be taking the sanctions off. It's time to do that.' He added that the relationship with Turkey was 'better' and that Turkey had been 'more helpful' than other NATO allies on Iran.

Trump said he had 'no concerns at all' about Turkey's possession of Russian S-400 air defense systems — a striking reversal from the position of his own first administration, which removed Turkey from the F-35 program in 2019 precisely because of the S-400. He said Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth were working on the sanctions removal.

Reports indicate Turkey is seeking an initial batch of six F-35 jets. Israel has pushed back hard — Netanyahu appeared on Fox News on Sunday and Monday to detail Turkey's 'anti-Israel rap sheet,' and Israeli analysts warned the sale would undermine Israel's qualitative military edge. Defense Secretary Hegseth, who was in Ankara for the summit, had planned a last-minute trip to Israel to discuss the matter with Netanyahu but called off the visit, according to Israeli media.

Congressional opposition remains a significant hurdle. US law currently prohibits F-35 sales to any country operating Russian air defense systems, and the original CAATSA sanctions were bipartisan. Jewish Insider reported that Trump's statement effectively pressures Congress but does not guarantee the sale will proceed.
sections.whyItMatters: If the F-35 sale goes through, it would be a historic policy reversal that reshapes the Eastern Mediterranean military balance. Turkey regains fifth-generation stealth capability, Israel's qualitative edge is diluted, and the S-400 coexistence precedent weakens NATO's Russia-sanctions posture. But congressional pushback makes this far from a done deal.

sections.heading: Imamoglu Ejected From Court as Erdogan Hosts World Leaders
sections.body:
On the same day Erdogan welcomed NATO leaders to Ankara, Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu was physically removed from his corruption trial at the Silivri court complex outside Istanbul. Imamoglu, the CHP's designated presidential candidate, had been barred from attending hearings for almost a week over what the court called 'disruptive behavior.' On Wednesday, he confronted the judge: 'How can you explain to world leaders at the NATO summit, in Turkey, in Ankara, the silencing of Ekrem Imamoglu?'

The case is the largest Imamoglu faces — a 3,900-page indictment alleging he led a criminal organization since 2015 that engaged in widespread corruption, bid rigging, and payoff schemes to finance his political rise. He faces 142 charges alongside 413 co-defendants. Imamoglu denies all charges and calls the prosecution politically motivated.

The optics were stark: while Erdogan projected statesmanship on the international stage, his most significant domestic rival was being silenced in a courtroom. The AP noted that hundreds of CHP members and elected officials have been detained over the past two years in what critics describe as a systematic effort to cripple Turkey's largest opposition party.
sections.whyItMatters: The juxtaposition of the NATO summit and Imamoglu's ejection crystallizes the dual narrative Turkey presents to the world: a militarily capable, diplomatically indispensable ally that also systematically constrains its democratic opposition. Western leaders attending the summit had to navigate that tension silently.

sections.heading: Trump Declares Iran Ceasefire 'Over,' Strikes Resume During Summit
sections.body:
President Trump declared the interim ceasefire with Iran 'over' during the NATO summit, following overnight Iranian strikes on US military installations in Bahrain and Kuwait. Those Iranian attacks came in retaliation for a CENTCOM operation that hit over 80 targets in Iran on July 7, including air-defense systems, command-and-control networks, coastal radar sites, and more than 60 IRGC small boats. The US strikes were themselves retaliation for Iranian attacks on three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.

Trump threatened further strikes on Wednesday night, saying 'we will probably hit them again.' NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte called the US attacks on Iran 'absolutely necessary.'

For Turkey, the resumption of hostilities creates a direct dilemma: Erdogan has positioned Turkey as a mediator in the Iran conflict, and the summit's agenda was disrupted by the very escalation Turkey had hoped to help resolve. Erdogan's earlier statement that 'Turkey chose diplomacy over escalation' now sits uneasily alongside a summit dominated by military strikes from Turkey's NATO ally against a neighbor with which Turkey shares intelligence and trade ties.

Cavusoglu, head of Turkey's intelligence agency, stated that Turkey would continue its mediation efforts regardless, according to Daily Sabah.
sections.whyItMatters: The collapse of the Iran ceasefire at the summit itself underscores how little control Turkey — or any NATO member — has over the trajectory of the US-Iran conflict. Erdogan's mediation pitch is harder to sustain when the summit he hosts becomes the stage for announcing new strikes.

sections.heading: Trump Removes Syria Sanctions, Meets al-Sharaa
sections.body:
In a dramatic sideline meeting at the NATO summit, President Trump met with Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa and announced the removal of all US sanctions on Syria and the revocation of Syria's designation as a 'state sponsor of terrorism.' Trump said: 'We lifted the sanctions on the country, and that was, in my opinion, a tremendous boost.' He praised al-Sharaa's governance and expressed optimism that Syria could 'help' deal with Hezbollah in Lebanon.

The move has major implications for Turkey, which has been the primary backer of the Syrian opposition and now sees its preferred partner in Damascus receiving direct US legitimization. Turkey has maintained forces in northern Syria and has sought to normalize relations with the new Syrian government. The removal of sanctions could open reconstruction opportunities for Turkish construction firms, but also complicates Turkey's position regarding the SDF/YPG forces it still considers a security threat along its southern border.
sections.whyItMatters: The US lifting of Syria sanctions directly serves Turkish interests — it removes a barrier to Turkish business involvement in Syrian reconstruction and reduces pressure on a government Turkey is trying to work with. But it also means the US is now directly shaping the Syrian endgame that Turkey previously considered its own project.

sections.heading: IMF Cuts Turkey's 2026 Growth Forecast to 2.9%
sections.body:
The International Monetary Fund lowered its 2026 growth forecast for Turkey from 3.4% to 2.9%, the second cut this year, citing the impact of the US-Iran conflict on commodity prices, trade disruptions from the Strait of Hormuz closure, and broader global economic headwinds. The IMF also cut its global growth projection to 3.0% for 2026, warning that elevated energy prices and supply chain disruptions from the Iran conflict were dragging down emerging markets.

The revision comes as Turkey's currency and inflation dynamics remain under pressure. According to Ekonomim, the dollar-lira rate and gold prices were closely tracked on July 8, with investors watching the Hormuz situation for further volatility. Turkey's heavy energy import dependence means any sustained disruption in Gulf shipping lanes directly impacts the current account.

The IMF's Middle East and Central Asia growth forecast was cut even more sharply — to 0.7% — reflecting the severe impact of the Hormuz closure on oil-exporting Gulf states, Iraq, and Kuwait. Turkey is positioned between these two dynamics: hurt as an energy importer but potentially benefiting from transit alternatives and reconstruction demand in post-conflict Syria.
sections.whyItMatters: The IMF cut quantifies the economic cost of the Iran conflict for Turkey. The 0.5 percentage-point downgrade is significant for an economy already managing persistent inflation and lira weakness. If Hormuz reopens, the outlook could improve — but the ceasefire collapse makes that uncertain.

bottomLine: The NATO Ankara Summit was a high-water mark for Erdogan's strategic positioning: Trump pledged to lift CAATSA sanctions and consider F-35 sales, Erdogan unveiled a $24 billion Steel Dome air defense project and committed Turkey to 3.5% GDP defense spending by 2030, and Trump's removal of Syria sanctions aligns with Turkish interests. But the same day underscored the limits of Erdogan's leverage — Trump declared the Iran ceasefire 'over' and launched new strikes during the summit itself, and Imamoglu's ejection from court reminded the world that Turkey's democratic credentials remain contested. The F-35 decision still faces congressional opposition, and the IMF cut Turkey's growth forecast to 2.9%, a reminder that the Iran conflict's economic spillover is not abstract for Ankara.
sources: AP News, Reuters, Jewish Insider, ABC News, Devdiscourse, Ilkha, 1news.az, Daily Sabah, Washington Post, Al Jazeera, Anadolu Agency, Ekonomim, New York Times, NBC News, RealClearPolitics, T24, SETA
